resources.string: Use this second screen to verify activity transitions and another view hierarchy in TV sessions.
Leanback-based browse rails and overlay templates aligned more closely with Android TV layout guidance.
Browse screens use a hero region plus vertically stacked content rails. Users move up and down between clusters, then left and right within each rail.
Use a strong focal area with concise metadata and actions before users drop into browse rows below.
Default stacked browse experience for large media catalogs.

Use browse as the default Android TV landing pattern when the user needs to move through multiple clusters of content. Lead with a featured area or header context, keep rows consistent, and make horizontal navigation inside each rail predictable.
Metadata and actions stay anchored on the left side of hero content.

A left overlay keeps primary text, status, and actions aligned to the reading direction while letting artwork or video remain visible on the right. This works well for hero states where users should immediately understand what they can do next.
Supports alternate emphasis when visual content should lead from the left.

Use a right overlay when the visual should enter from the left and supporting metadata should stay out of the way. Keep actions grouped and avoid making the overlay so wide that it competes with the hero asset.
Best for hero-first promotional or playback-entry states.

Center overlays are best for promotional, spotlight, or playback-entry moments where the current item is the entire focus. Because centered text can obscure more content, use it sparingly and keep the message short.
Keeps contextual details below the focal area without obscuring imagery.

Bottom overlays are useful when users need more metadata, progress, or actions without covering key visual content. This pattern works well for details-heavy hero treatments and post-selection expansion states.
Card width changes how editorial versus catalog-like the row feels.
Stable widths and consistent row spacing make D-pad movement predictable.
These widths reflect the official 1-to-5-card browse layout patterns, including the 844dp single-card treatment.
The 1-card layout acts like a featured editorial rail. Use it when a single item deserves full emphasis and the row should feel like a hero continuation rather than a dense catalog.
A 2-card row balances emphasis and choice. It works well for paired recommendations, dual editorial picks, or simple category entry points where each tile still needs strong visual weight.
The 3-card layout is a versatile midpoint. It offers enough density for browsing while still giving each card room for artwork and readable metadata.
A 4-card row starts to feel catalog-oriented. Use it for common browse rails where users expect to scan multiple options quickly with consistent focus movement.
The 5-card layout favors breadth over emphasis. Reserve it for larger libraries or utility rails where showing more items at once matters more than highlighting a single choice.
Overlay treatments position metadata around a focused hero area while preserving readable content and clear action placement.
TV actions should be clear, high contrast, and easy to focus with the D-pad. Keep the primary action visually dominant and avoid crowded button clusters.
Tabs work best for a small number of closely related destinations. Keep labels short and make the selected state obvious from a distance.
Dialogs should interrupt the user only for important confirmations or short decisions. Place focus on the safest default action and keep the message concise.
Official TV guidance emphasizes stable focus order, clear groupings, and minimal surprises when moving with the remote. Users should always understand where focus will go next.
Use this screen as a fast visual playground before moving to richer Leanback or Compose TV components.
Keep focus movement obvious with clear rows and stable card widths.
Reserve denser rails for browsing and wider cards for hero or editorial content.
Treat overlays as supporting structure around the currently focused content.
A wide metadata panel sits to the left of the hero, ideal for synopsis, labels, and primary actions.
Use sparingly for promotional states where text and actions are the primary focal point.
Moves supporting information to the right while the hero image remains dominant.
Good for extra metadata, actions, and contextual details below the focal content area.
Set the Capture API key and URL directly on the TV app, then save to start the SDK.
Save valid settings to start Capture for the first time.
Settings saved. Enter a valid API key and URL to start Capture.
Capture is not configured. Open Settings and add the API key and URL.
